Are you using determinism in your facial landmark script? You have to call `aug_det = aug.to_deterministic()` before augmenting the image and landmarks. Otherwise, a different rotation might be applied to image and landmarks.
Below is (mostly) the script from above, but modified with determinism:

```python
from __future__ import print_function, division
import imgaug as ia
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from scipy import misc, ndimage
import numpy as np

ia.seed(57)

# simple function to draw a keypoint on an image
def drawpoint(img, kp, size=4):
    h, w = img.shape[0:2]
    x = kp.x
    y = kp.y
    x1 = x - size
    x2 = x
    y1 = y - size
    y2 = y
    if 0 <= x1 < w and 0 <= x2 < w and 0 <= y1 < h and 0 <= y2 < h:
        img[y1:y2, x1:x2, 0] = 255
    return img

s = 150  # size of the image
pd = 25  # positions of each corner pixel (e.g. at (25, 25), (-25, 25), ...)
ps = 6  # size of each square to draw
img = np.zeros((s, s, 3), dtype=np.uint8)  # black example image
# add white squares in the corners
img[pd - ps:pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 255
img[-pd - ps:-pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 128
img[pd - ps:pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 64
img[-pd - ps:-pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 255

# define one test keypoint at (100, 100)
kp_x = 100
kp_y = 100
kps = [ia.KeypointsOnImage([ia.Keypoint(kp_x, kp_y)], shape=img.shape)]
img_with_kp = np.copy(img)
img_with_kp[kp_y - ps:kp_y, kp_x - ps:kp_x, 2] = 255
img_org2 = kps[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(img_with_kp))

nb_augs = 1000  # how often to augment here
aug_seq = iaa.Sequential({
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-60, 60))  # rotate -40 to 40 deg
})
aug_aff = iaa.Affine(rotate=(-60, 60))

###############
# make deterministic
###############
aug_seq = aug_seq.to_deterministic()
aug_aff = aug_aff.to_deterministic()
###############

# augment images
imgs_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_image(img_with_kp)
imgs_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_image(img_with_kp)

# augment keypoints
kps_seq_aug = []
kps_aff_aug = []
kps_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_keypoints(kps)
kps_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_keypoints(kps)
seq_x, seq_y = kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].y
aff_x, aff_y = kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].y

# code to test draw_on_image
img_org = kps[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(img_with_kp))
img_seq_aug = kps_seq_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_seq_aug))
img_aff_aug = kps_aff_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_aff_aug))
img_seq_aug2 = drawpoint(np.copy(img_seq_aug), kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0])
img_aff_aug2 = drawpoint(np.copy(img_aff_aug), kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0])
# misc.imshow(np.hstack([img_seq_aug, img_aff_aug, img_org.astype(np.uint8)]))

# plot
misc.imsave("test_image2.png", np.hstack([img_seq_aug2, img_aff_aug2, img_org, img_org2]))
```

![test_image2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11698516/28168317-fa08247c-67de-11e7-9b50-7491ed5dfb43.png)

Images are the same as before.
Blue = keypoint, but drawn on the image before augmentation. So it is modified by augment_image() instead of augment_keypoints()
Green = keypoint after augmentation, drawn by draw_on_image()
Red = keypoint after augmentation, drawn by drawkeypoint(), which uses a similar drawing technique as the blue keypoint.

I think that the primary reason for FoBiS is to build Fortran code, and that not much good will come from forcing poor fortran stefano to learn C++.  

From what I understand, even with iso_c_bind, the process of building some fortran calling c does not actually involve any interaction between the sources. It is only at the linking stage that there is any contact between the langauges, so it seems to me that it is in fact just a special case of using an external library. 

So it seem that to handle the case where Fortran code depends on libraries in other languages FoBiS should have the ability to quite blindly invoke some external build system (specified by the user) that in turn builds the library. I recall there being some discussion about how to automatically rebuild external fortran langauges, so I guess a good start would be to look at that and see if you could just slap in an execution of some user specified shell command.

9. What does being a conda-forge feedstock maintainer entail?
The maintainers "job" is to:
- keep the feedstock updated by merging eventual maintenance PRs from conda-forge's bots;

I've found a way around this through use of an S3 'bucket policy'. This sets a rule in the bucket to add the `public-read` canned ACL on objects, and thus makes object URLs publicly accessible.

Here's the bucket policy I've used to achieve this:

```json
{
	"Version": "2012-10-17",
	"Statement": [
		{
			"Sid": "AddPerm",
			"Effect": "Allow",
			"Principal": "*",
			"Action": "s3:GetObject",
			"Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::mybucketname/uploads/*"
		}
	]
}
```

In this example, all objects inside the `uploads` directory of the bucket called `mybucketname` are public.

-----

That aside, I would still be interested to know whether there are any provisions within `yas3fs` to set ACLs on objects, similar to the way it's done with `s3fs`. But in the meantime, this bucket policy seems to be a nice workaround.

I like the idea of supporting "hidden" values or "id's" or "keys" (whatever they need to be called) for ButtonGroup, Combo and ListBox. They are particularly useful when presenting values from lookup lists, enums or dicts. i.e. the text shown to user is different to the id which is used internally.

An approach would be that widgets can accept either:
+ a 1d list of items which would be shown to the user and returned by `value`
+ a 2d list of items and keys, the item would be shown to the user and the key returned by `value`

e.g.

```python
combo1 = Combo(a, ["mr", "mrs", "miss"], selected="mr")
assert combo1.value == "mr"

combo2 = Combo(a, [["mr", "0"], ["mrs", "1"], ["miss", "2"]], selected="1")
assert combo2.value == "1"
```

This is similar to ButtonGroup, but at the moment if you setup ButtonGroup with a 1d list of values it creates its own set of keys (starting at "1") rather than using the item text.

I suspect the vast majority of people would use a 1d list as returning the value you set seems a simpler model and would fulfil most people's needs.

What array are you talking about? The `$PhysicalNames` section does not contain arrays, just integers and strings.

Look at the example below. The line `2 1 "outer"`  means that `"outer"` is the physical name of a surface labeled with `1`, while `3 5 "domain"` corresponds to a volume labeled with `5`. 

* Input `.geo` file
```
...
Physical Volume  ("domain",5) = {domain[]};
Physical Surface ("outer",1) = {outer[]};
Physical Surface ("inner",2) = {inner[]};
...
```

* Output ASCII `.msh` file
```
$MeshFormat
2.2 0 8
$EndMeshFormat
$PhysicalNames
3
2 1 "outer"
2 2 "inner"
3 5 "domain"
$EndPhysicalNames
...
```

If backward compatibility is not an issue, then I propose that `meshio` should return a `field_data` dict like this `{"outer": (1, 2), "inner": (2,2), "domain": (3, 5)}`, i.e. `key: "physical-name"`, `value: (physical-number, physical-dimension)`.

From our experience, however, we have not been happier to have built our integrated design framework on top of python/gdspy and have cut out all the other layout dependencies from our workflow. This allowed us to fully automate our design and fabrication cycle and collaborate more effectively in spite of running on different OSs and hardware.
The “jogs” that you may have observed are intended. Our waveguide rendering tool compensates for the displacement of the mode in a curved waveguide relative to the waveguide center. In cases when the curvature of the waveguide changes abruptly, the renderer will produce a kink in the waveguide to align the modes in the waveguides with different curvatures.

Here is a working heart rate example.

You will obviously need to change the address.

```python
from btle import Peripheral, ADDR_TYPE_RANDOM, AssignedNumbers

import time

class HRM(Peripheral):
    def __init__(self, addr):
        Peripheral.__init__(self, addr, addrType=ADDR_TYPE_RANDOM)

if __name__=="__main__":
    cccid = AssignedNumbers.client_characteristic_configuration
    hrmid = AssignedNumbers.heart_rate
    hrmmid = AssignedNumbers.heart_rate_measurement

    hrm = None
    try:
        hrm = HRM('cb:d7:a0:40:c4:01')

        service, = [s for s in hrm.getServices() if s.uuid==hrmid]
        ccc, = service.getCharacteristics(forUUID=str(hrmmid))

        if 0: # This doesn't work
            ccc.write('\1\0')

        else:
            desc = hrm.getDescriptors(service.hndStart,
                                      service.hndEnd)
            d, = [d for d in desc if d.uuid==cccid]
            
            hrm.writeCharacteristic(d.handle, '\1\0')
        
        t0=time.time()
        def print_hr(cHandle, data):
            bpm = ord(data[1])
            print bpm,"%.2f"%(time.time()-t0)
        hrm.delegate.handleNotification = print_hr

        for x in range(10):
            hrm.waitForNotifications(3.)

    finally:
        if hrm:
            hrm.disconnect()
```

>  We are already able to determine memory leaks by using Process.memory_info().rss or Process.memory_full_info().uss before and after a function call. 

See https://bugs.python.org/issue33444. A higher `rss` tells you that there may be a memory leak, not that there is one. Python uses `malloc()` for all allocations > 512 bytes. When Python releases memory to the glibc (by calling `free()`), the glibc isn't always able to return memory to the system, so `rss` appears to remain high.

> Also, it seems mallinfo() is basically deprecated

I see, perhaps `malloc_info` is more worth exposing then? Though it's return values aren't documented...

In addition to the fixes I also wrote a quick unit test to mirror the falsy values one I just added, but for Django REST Framework. I didn't commit it since I didn't want to make DRF and Django a dependency, but if you wanted to confirm that the behaviour is the same, here it is:

```python
from django.conf import settings
settings.configure()

import django
django.setup()

from .obj import Obj
from rest_framework import serializers
import unittest


class TestDRFCompat(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_behaviour_with_falsy_values(self):
        class ASerializer(serializers.Serializer):
            a = serializers.IntegerField(required=False)
            b = serializers.BooleanField(required=False)
            c = serializers.CharField()
            d = serializers.CharField(required=False)

        o = Obj(a=0, b=False, c="", d="")
        data = ASerializer(o).data
        self.assertTrue('a' in data)
        self.assertTrue('b' in data)
        self.assertTrue('c' in data)
        self.assertTrue('d' in data)
```

The test case needed is along these lines:
```
In [1]: import datetime

In [2]: import ofxtools

In [3]: converter = ofxtools.Types.DateTime()

In [4]: def roundtrip(dt_foreign):
   ...:     return converter.unconvert(converter.convert(dt_foreign))
   ...: 
   ...: 

In [5]: def tripround(dt_python):
   ...:     return converter.convert(converter.unconvert(dt_python))
   ...: 
   ...: 

In [6]: roundtrip('20180601010000')
Out[6]: '20180601010000'

In [7]: tripround(datetime.datetime(2018,6,1,1))
Out[7]: datetime.datetime(2018, 5, 31, 17, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)
```
The problem here is that `Types.DateTime.unconvert()` assumes TZ-naive `datetime`s are local time; it actually needs to default to UTC.  This would at least be a consistent representation of my starting posture towards timezones, i.e. an OFX library has little business handling them, and should just use UTC internally.

TZ data enters the library either from OFX server responses, or client applications handing in Python datatypes.  Well-formed OFX responses are trivial to timezone correct; they always specify GMT offset unambiguously per the spec.  It's true that incoming Python datatypes do need to deal with TZ headaches, but surely that responsibility can and should be delegated to the application that's dealing with user input, DB persistence, foreign code, or what have you.

It might be better to have a stricter API, and require that input to `Types.DateTime.unconvert()` be a TZ-aware `datetime` instance (rather than accepting naive `datetime`s and also `date` instances, as at present).  That'd be much clearer.

I'm reluctant to add dependencies to `ofxtools.`  What would importing `pytz` really buy us?  Honest question.

AFAICT, having `pytz` available would offer two potential benefits:
1. It would help `Types.DateTime.convert()` work around buggy OFX server implementations (e.g. pre-2014 Interactive Brokers OFX downloads, which left the GMT offset blank but included TZ name) ... but this is frankly pretty low priority; and
2. It would make `Client.py` more robust.  The use of local time for user input is somewhat problematic; it would really be better to use the TZ of the OFX server, which could be included in the config file.  This is where you need a TZ library like `pytz`.

Anything else?

Hello @amankumarjain,
That's actually a really nice question that I tried to solve some times ago. My conclusion was:
1. Using celery is not really a solution (at least for me as we are using SQS as a queue system) because response time of the DB ~= response time of SQS. So not a solution
2. Async python3 could be a way to do it, for I don't remember which reason, we could consider this solution so I don't know
3. "Send a request to add data and do not wait for the answer" was our best solution. What it means is you do your call to what ever system is saving your data but you don't wait the answer or there is no answer. By doing that you avoid every latency or service unavailable disruption. The bad point of this solution is that you could potentially lose data, or at least, not register them and don't know about it.

Actually the solution we went for, with a nice scaling possibility, is the Elasticsearch integration. We have an ES cluster hosted on Elastic cloud and we are sending our data directly to ES. An improvement to do is the 3rd point that I told you before, to send without waiting for the answer. 

So I don't think there is an easy or awesome solution but there is definitively things to try. Aside all the possibilities, I think that DRF is trying to keep the impact on the API very low. If I'm not mistaken, our tests are checking that we don't take more than 20ms to log something (which is not perfect but it's ok-ish)

I think it actually is supposed to be 2017 based on the conversation in #99. The default behavior is that "June 15" parses as June 15 2016 (since we have already passed that date in 2015) so "next" adds one year after that. There are a few settings (YearParseStyle, DOWParseStyle and CurrentDOWParseStyle) on Constants that can be used to make "next" and "last" relative to the current calendar year instead.

The issue with this is that "Tuesday" and "Dec 22nd" are both valid dates so the parser will always return a date that satisfies all of the date expressions found.

I do like the idea of checking to see if any modifiers are present but we then still have the "which date wins" choice to make :/

@v-chojas The reason I like the cache is I have large applications today that use a lot of SQL statements and not a single one uses SQLDescribeParam except when inserting `None`, and that happens automatically.

By switching to use SQLDescribeParam all the time, I'm concerned there is going to be a big performance hit.  Obviously we should test this.  With a large enough cache, I think many applications could end up caching almost all of their statements, except for dynamically generated ones, and pay for the describe step once.

I'd want a way to mark a particular execute so it doesn't go into the cache - the apps I'm thinking of are using a home grown ORM and it would be useful to turn those off.

I guess the next step would be to make a branch with SQLDescribeParam and no cache and measure performance?

Hi,

You need to pass `store=True` to cause the object to be written to the device. See http://python-pkcs11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pkcs11.Session.generate_key

The reason for this is to create some consistency between the PKCS#11 libraries, which will randomly store objects or not, with no consistency so we default a value (some still ignore the parameter, hooray PKCS#11). You'll also need to open your session read/write to create a non session lifetime object.

The blog post will not come. After having looked at all *live* data feeds and although a pattern is more or less there, the next provider would need specific development depending on how data and event are delivered.

But the raw summary is:

  - The `_load` function of the feed is a finite state machine

The following states play a role:

  - `_ST_OVER`: the feed can no longer deliver data. This may be due to a number of circumstances, like having specified the wrong contract or having actually exhausted the data after a historical-only download

  - `_ST_FROM`: an additional data feed is being used for the initial backfill. The feed will be pushed forward until exhausted and the data copied to `self` during each iteration

  - `_ST_HISTORBACK`: used for backfilling and historical only downloads (parameter `historical` is set to `True`)

  - `_ST_LIVE`: The state in which live data is being received. Of course here the detection of problems notified by the *store* plays a role

    There is an additional substate held in `_statelivereconn` to allow the feed to notify the strategy when the new data (after a reconnection) is `LIVE` (a buffer of 1 is pending processing)

In `_ST_LIVE` and `_ST_HISTORBACK` the received data is passed to auxiliary functions that load the data into the lines of the feed.

Connectivity is handled by the corresponding store (in this case `OandaStore`). The store is in charge managing the actual connection model and data reception to the provider.

The data feed sits on a `queue.Queue` object waiting for the *store* to push data into it and needs no thread management to listen to anything (for example a socket)

It is sensible to think that a generic live feed and live broker could define generic interfaces for a, as much as possible, streamlined integration from providers.

But connection to providers was added very late into *backtrader* and hence the lack of such an interface.

Hi @vahidm! You're right, `django-crontab` does not handle this - but that is correct. It's the task of the operating system that is running cron. It will (or better: should) run 2 individual processes, each's stdout pointing to the same file. Depending on the exact execution start, things like context/process switching and stdout buffering the order of the jobs logging may vary, but it should be there.

I have a very bad assumption of what's maybe your problem. The README contains examples of how to configure the `CRONJOBS` setting to log the job output into a file. And in this example the `>` operator is used that overwrites the file - this is of course misleading. It should be ">>" which will append the output to the file. Did your use ">" from the example?

```
parglare.exceptions.ParseError: Error at position 4,4 => " real\n    *float is a". Expected: EOF or define or eol
```

In the debug output, it first correctly reduces`typelines : typeline ;` as follows:
```
Current state: 11
	Context:  real\n    *float is a
	Tokens expected: EOF or NAMETK or define or eol
```

In the next state it reduces to `typedefinition`, which is wrong, imho, since after a `typedefinition`, you can only have another type definition (which starts with a `"define" keyword), you can have an empty line (`eol`), or you can have  `EOF`. In particular, you cannot have a `NAMETK`.
```
Current state: 10
	Context:  real\n    *float is a
	Tokens expected: EOF or NAMETK or define or eol
	Token ahead: <NAMETK(float)>
	Reducing by prod '5: typedefinition = typedefheader typelines'.

	Action result = type:<class 'list'> value:[[[[], '\n'], 'define', 'type', '\n'], [[], 'real', '\n']]
```

So I fail to understand how it reaches this decision based on `NAMETK` look-ahead.
State 10 is as follows:
```
State 10:typelines
	5: typedefinition = typedefheader typelines .   {eol, define, EOF, NAMETK}
	10: typelines = typelines . typeline   {eol, define, EOF, NAMETK}

	13: empties = .   {eol, NAMETK}

	GOTO:

	ACTIONS:
	define->REDUCE:5, eol->[REDUCE:5,REDUCE:13], EOF->REDUCE:5, NAMETK->[REDUCE:5,REDUCE:13]
```

Hi, I would sorry to say that both 'required' and 'readOnly' for Schema object is not implemented yet in pyswagger, I would include these fix in next release.

I'm a little bit busy these days so can't dig into this issue more deep, but I think a property which is both 'required' and 'readOnly' is kind of conflict, and it seems that the spec says it's not legal, too

> Relevant only for Schema "properties" definitions. Declares the property as "read only". This means that it MAY be sent as part of a response but MUST NOT be sent as part of the request. Properties marked as readOnly being true SHOULD NOT be in the required list of the defined schema. Default value is false.

(refer to https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields-13)

In your use case, I looked for similar case in issues list of swagger spec, and got this https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/228, and per webron's comment, they discussed more fine grained control, and settle down to current version. IMO, just specify 'readOnly' is enough in your case.

经试验后发现是分词的原因，`jieba` 分词后的结果是 `还没` 被拆分成了 `还` 和 `没`，所以词组拼音没有被应用。两种解决办法:

1. 使用字符串列表作为参数，手动分词:

```python
>>> pinyin('还没')
[['huán'], ['méi']]
>>> pinyin(['还没'])
[['hái'], ['méi']]
```
2. 卸载或禁用 ``jieba`` 分词，最新版的 pypinyin 已内置简单的分词功能. 详见文档 [1] [2]

[1] http://pypinyin.readthedocs.org/en/master/#tips
[2] http://pypinyin.readthedocs.org/en/master/#id11

I'm also getting it from time to time since many months ago on both Facebook and Twitter. It's not related to mobile or slow connections. It seldom happened to me, but when it does, I go back, click the login button again and then it works. In production it happens like 5 times a day every day, always to different people.

I'll add the results of my research:
* This is not about the URL. *Don't look for a missing parameter "state" in the URL*. There is a `&state=…` parameter in OAuth2, and also a `&redirect_state=…`, but they work all right when this bug happens. What PSA is complaining about is a state parameter *inside session*, which is called `facebook_state` or `twitter_state` 
* It happened but with OAuth2 (Facebook) and very rarely with OAuth1 (Twitter). Always in the /complete/ step
* It always happens when logging in (that means the user cannot log in and stays anonimous) except a very rare occasion when it happened on logout
* You can reproduce it easily by going to a URL which has an expired token, although I'm not sure the conditions are the same as the bug in the wild. E.g. `http://www.yourdomain.com/account/complete/twitter/?redirect_state=8edOO0H4AcNXohUB7IOOYRaiEhv6iK4b&oauth_token=E1DsnqYwM6ie0U5Zd2RqHsOixWqOZgQh&oauth_verifier=0VeL60uek54y1pWgAmQegysG1lwW0IFN`
* If I look at the session data at the time of the error (in a middleware), I confirm that the session doesn't have a `twitter_state parameter`. It has: `[('currency', 'IDR'), ('django_language', 'id')]`, while in a normal (non-failing) login, there is a `twitter_state` parameter: `[(u'twitterunauthorized_token_name', [u'oauth_token=rh19NS7EkL3Wovwn9Mi8KbM24ZLDfOt7&oauth_token_secret=3NON79A7UYYD5Ec73SB0p1c8f3M7Igfk&oauth_callback_confirmed=true']), (u'next', u'/'), (u'currency', u'IDR'), (u'twitter_state', u'75N2XDSQnyIOaKEBV6RsofkmcLTKyDDG'), (u'_language', u'id'), (u'django_language', u'id')]`
* I'm using memcached and nothing too strange.
* I'm suspicious that this bug is because: the user starts logging in and uses the auth_token etc, then something goes wrong and the browser tries to load the URL again. Fb/Twitter doesn't allow to use those tokens a 2nd time, and therefore the /complete/ step doesn't have all the necessary information in session.

Any comments are appreciated! I want to add more debug information or some workaround for this.

def restore_windows_1252_characters(s):
    """Replace C1 control characters in the Unicode string s by the
    characters at the corresponding code points in Windows-1252,
    where possible.

    """
    import re
    def to_windows_1252(match):
        try:
            return bytes([ord(match.group(0))]).decode('windows-1252')
        except UnicodeDecodeError:
            # No character at the corresponding code point: remove it.
            return ''
    return re.sub(r'[\u0080-\u0099]', to_windows_1252, s)

    def connect(self, primary_email, server=None, version=None):
        if primary_email in self.targets and self.targets[primary_email] is not None:
            return self.targets[primary_email]
        try:
            if server:
                if version is not None:
                    #print ('using pre-defined version (no auto discover)')
                    aver = version.split('.')
                    if len(aver)<4:
                        #print ("error: version must be of format v.v.v.v")
                        return False
                    version = Version(build=Build(aver[0], aver[1], aver[2], aver[3]))
                    config = Configuration(server=server, credentials=self.credentials, version=version, auth_type=NTLM)
                else:
                    config = Configuration(server=server, credentials=self.credentials, has_ssl=True)
                account = Account(primary_smtp_address=primary_email, config=config, autodiscover=False,

    def special_chars(self, s):
        if False:

            print (s)
            print ("##################")
        x = []
        skip = 0
        for i in range(0, len(s)-3):
            if skip>0:
                skip -= 1
                continue
            if s[i] == 0xe2 and s[i+1]==0x80 and (s[i+2]==0x99 or s[i+2]==0x98):
                x.append("'")
                skip = 2
            elif s[i] == 0xe2 and s[i+1]==0x80: # and (s[i+2]==0x99 or s[i+2]==0x98):
                x.append("-")
                skip = 2
            elif s[i] == 0xf0 and s[i+1] == 0x9f: #\x98\x8a
                x.append("-")
                skip = 3
            else:
                x.append(chr(s[i]))
        x.append(chr(s[len(s)-2]))
        x.append(chr(s[len(s)-1]))
        return (''.join(x)).encode('utf8')

The edge-case prompting our work-around for inexact `ContentType`s is for some dynamically-generated classes (see bottom): https://github.com/edoburu/django-fluent-contents/blob/master/fluent_contents/plugins/markup/models.py

Basically, we have dynamically-generated class variants of the base `MarkupItem` such as `MarkdownMarkupItem` and `TextileMarkupItem`, but when you look up their ContentType you get the `MarkupItem` class which does not match the more exact/correct version (e.g. `MarkdownMarkupItem`) set on the `Formset.model` the method matches against.

Admittedly this is all pretty unusual stuff, and might only ever be useful for this particular CMS. Maybe the best option is to move the class comparison into a method so we can easily override it to handle our use-case? Or we could just override the `get_related_versions` implementation wholesale and leave the original unchanged, which is a bit messier but only for us ;)

`ext` is the file extension (including the '.')
`path` is the directory containing the file (an empty string in my case)
`file_name` is the file name without the extension

The second line (line 120) is used to remove the extension. os.path.split does not separate the extension, os.path.splitext does

I believe it’s needed: if we stopped at line 119, we would have the extension still present in file_name, which we don’t want because we want to insert “thumbnail” between the file name and its extension.

It gets complicated. When followed by a single name part, there's actually a [set of titles](http://nameparser.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modules.html#module-nameparser.config.titles) which are followed by first names (Uncle Bob, Queen Mary) and a separate set followed by last names (Admiral Cartwright, Officer Johnson), the larger set is currently expecting last names to follow .

But that makes me think, actually there's actually a few things in that titles constant that should only be title parts if they are followed by another title part, meant to be chained together. For example "Grand" is only a title when it's followed by another title part, "duke" or "duchess" or a few others. But never in your case where it's not followed by another title.

I have also commented, in the current documentation, on some other places that may need looking at - search for "TODO" (which I perhaps should have indexed...). For instance, it's actually possible for a "homologous" node to contribute more to genomic distance than a non-homologous node, even if the disjoint coefficient is greater than the weight coefficient. (If it is the same, and likely too high, one gets the behavior that caused iznn to take forever in the test scripts - the population gets divided into lots and lots of species with only 2 genomes each, thanks to min_species_size.) As another instance, it's actually possible for the current stagnation setup to remove species containing genomes that weren't making progress because they were at the maximum possible fitness...

Admittedly, proper configuration will help with the above (although eliminating the first would be rather difficult, if one wants to keep a wide range of weights, biases, and/or response multipliers possible, for instance).  Some of the others, such as bias not being part of the distance calculation for iznn, are more matters for "how do we fix this with backward compatibility maximized but forward utility also maximized" than necessarily difficult to code.

Okay, it looks like the issue is a result of two things:

 1. There are multiple zip codes that Wausau, WI covers.
 2. The Google geocoder seems to either ignore the request for a specific zip code, or is getting confused and just returning a blanket result for Wausau that does not include *any* of the possible zip codes.

This is a bit disappointing to say the least, I'd hoped that sourcing Google's geocode data would eliminate these kinds of issues, but sadly that doesn't appear to be the case.

So, I can think of a couple of possible solutions.

 1. Use a different geocoder. The issue with this is obviously that there's no guarantee other geocoders are any better.
 2. Store the raw address given to the geocoder as the formatted address. This will keep the zip code in the text, but we still won't have the zip code as a logical component of the address hierarchy, which is still not great. You could manually add it in, but it's not an ideal solution that's for sure.

It sounds like you've hit a bug in sphinx's latex writer not being able to handle citations in figure captions. The extension itself is not concerned with any specific backends and simply generates reference nodes (as standard rest citations would).

Anyway, my guess is that sphinx uses phantomsection to permit back-references. If latex doesn't support these inside captions and you really rely on this, then I suggest that you override the writer with one that doesn't support hypertargets; from a quick scan of the sphinx source code, you just need to override the LaTeXTranslator.hypertarget function to return an empty string:

https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/blob/17e38457049ddbf3e069cd033b119db0e80addca/sphinx/writers/latex.py#L332

Another solution is of course not to have references (or any other hypertargets) inside captions.

I like the idea and I agree it would be useful to have, but I highly doubt it's possible. Especially in a generic way.

The big problem with bulk updates and bulk creates is that you don't really know which models you're updating, because that question is answered internally by your database. How do you know what data you should insert into Elasticsearch afterwards? Retrieve the updated models, convert them to objects and pass those through the regular handlers? That would defeat the purpose of bulk actions.

I've written something similar for my own projects, but I haven't released it yet as I did not yet get to a good solution (maybe still should?).

The main complexity comes from the fact that the partial "where conditions" may contain fields which are not listed in the Index's `fields` parameter. For example, consider the `Job` model's unique index on `group where is_complete = false`: https://github.com/mattiaslinnap/django-partial-index/blob/master/tests/testapp/models.py#L28

Following Django's form validation for standard full unique constraints, it would check if another row with the same `group` exists in the database, and raise error if yes. For the partial unique constraint validation, we have to raise the error *only if also `is_complete = 0` on the new* object. Because you can insert a duplicate group if the *new* object doesn't match the where condition, even if some existing ones do. But parsing the referenced fields from the text-based where conditions is tricky. 

The best solution I found so far is to actually run the `object.save()` during validation in a temporary transaction, turn any IntegrityError into ValidationError, and rollback on success. But this is not acceptable if models override `.save()` to modify fields before save, have pre_save/post_save signals, or similar.

I think a good solution would need replacing the "where conditions" with Django Q-expressions, or alternatively a "where referenced fields" parameter for the PartialIndex?
In essence, we need a way to determine "will this new object match the partial index condition" before actually saving it to the DB.

The important difference here is that the second argument in `parser.get('Section', 'option')` is *not* a fallback as in `dict.get('key', 'fallback')`. From the [documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/configparser.html#mapping-protocol-access): > `parser.get(section, option, **kwargs)` - the second argument is not a fallback value. Note however that the section-level get() methods are compatible both with the mapping protocol and the classic configparser API. Just for you two, I actually stepped through to figure out what was happening.

`S1` and `C1` are well conditioned. It is the result of the matrix multiplications and addition that is illconditionned. Typically this is caught a little lower

```python
        # M*|a b c >=l|a b c >. Find eigenvalues and eigenvectors
        # from this equation [eqn. 28]
        eig_vals, eig_vecs = np.linalg.eig(M)

        # eigenvector must meet constraint 4ac - b^2 to be valid.
        cond = 4 * np.multiply(eig_vecs[0, :], eig_vecs[2, :]) \
               - np.power(eig_vecs[1, :], 2)
        a1 = eig_vecs[:, (cond > 0)]
        # seeks for empty matrix
        if 0 in a1.shape or len(a1.ravel()) != 3:
            return False
```
On 64 bit:
```
(Pdb) cond
array([-0.28717554, -0.71271829, -0.00108459])
```
On 32 bit
```
(Pdb) cond
array([-1.38708836,  0.84396406, -0.03172746])
```

It just happened that on 64 bit the error didn't show up. But I don't think that the `eig` algorithm will complain if you have a ill-conditionned matrix.

Therefore, the tests both in *64 bit* and *32 bit* are caught by my added logic.

> Conflict handling on creation with deleted items doesn't makes sense.

I agree. An example of this situation would be:
* the resource does not exist
* client1 successfully creates the record, then successfully deletes it again, before client2 discovers it ever existed.
* client2 "thinks" the resource never existed, and tries to create it, possibly with the same content as what client1 put into it, possibly with different content.
* I agree client2's action should succeed in this case.

A way to see it is that the versioning of a resource should always be a linear step-by-step path, so that there is only one clear sequence of versions the resource goes through on the server, from its creation to its deletion. But once the resource has been deleted, this chain is forgotten by the server and it's back to its default state of non-existence.

At least that's the way we did it in remoteStorage.

The reasoning in #249 is different because that's based on modified-timestamps, not versions. There tombstones made more sense, maybe. But if you just look at ETags, then my intuition is to consider the lifespan of a resource from creation to deletion, and once it's gone, its lifespan is over and it can be forgotten.

@aw-bib, you should note that  *more frequent releases* is disjoint from *new features*. In Invenio packages carefully apply [semver](http://semver.org/) then in principle we can have one release per bugfix (thus increasing the minor version), and you can apply these on production without worry (as you do with any other deb/rpm package in your production system). You just need to limit dependencies to minor versions. E.g. you say `invenio-records==1.0.*`. You won't have scary new features, but you'll benefit bug/security fixes.

Previously, mswest46 (Michael West) wrote…
I thought about how to do this, but couldn't quite make it work. Currently, the user (maker of suite) is responsible for adding the test classes and configs, and then selecting which tests they want to run with a list of strings, in that order. Your suggestion requires the user to call the parser method, retrieve the methods that belong to particular instances test_class(config), and pass those methods in when they call add_test_class(test_class, config, test_methods). This seems like extra work/thought from the user. An alternative is to write something that adds tests in bulk (kind of like the old suite_runner did, but with multi-config file options.) Something like: ``` def add_test_classes_in_bulk(list(tuple) test_classes_and_configs, list(str) test_selections) ``` In this case, the user would just need to create a list of class/config pairs, and pass in the parsed test_selection strings. We would do the selection (and validation) before adding the classes to the test runner. Does that make sense? I might be being a bit thick here, but I don't see how to use the existing methods without making the creator of the suite think more than they have to.
The idea is to keep the test runner generic so different users can use it differently. Your parser function can return a format that makes it easy to call the TestRunner API with. e.g. it can return a list of tuples with the first element being the class and the second element being the specific tests to run, then it's a simple loop to call `add_test_class`. In pyunit if you want to create a suite, you follow a similar pattern. Building the parsing directly into the test runner also means we are gonna have to deal with all kinds of filtering pattern within test runner, which can quickly become something hard to manage bc different users are gonna want to filter their test sets differently. For the suite owner it is also easier to maintain python statements than a long string specifying what is what. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/352)*

@imranashraf ATM I don't need a sophisticated script, just open some default tabs for some specific folders, not like continuing the session from the last point. So here's the script to create some default tabs:
```
#!/bin/bash
sleep 3

# rename the initial tab for home scripting
guake -r "home"

# new tab 1
guake -n "/absolute/path/to/first/folder"
guake -r "tab name 1"

# new tab 2
guake -n "/absolute/path/to/second/folder"
guake -r "tab name 2"
```
Be care because the path doesn't follow symlinks, the path must be absolute.
Enjoy!

OK, I made a first attempt at a more scala-like interface. It's not complete (and not documented), but you can see it here:

https://github.com/allenai/dynet/tree/scaly/swig/src/main/scala/edu/cmu/dynet

At a high level, the changes are

* moved the SWIG bindings into `edu.cmu.dynet.internal` (but otherwise left them untouched)
* created a bunch of Scala wrapper classes, each of which wraps a package-private instance of the "internal" (SWIG-generated) Java class, and has methods that delegate to that instance. Basically, no one who uses this API should ever see/know about any of the SWIG-generated Java classes
* gave everything scala-like names
* simplified the `_Vector` wrappers and made them implement `IndexedSeq` so that they mostly "feel like" natural scala collections
* replaced the `dim()` helper function with factory methods in the `Dim` object
* put the singleton `ComputationGraph` in the `ComputationGraph` object, and moved all its methods to methods on that object
* accordingly, removed `ComputationGraph` from all function signatures
* versioned `Expression`s (as in the python code)
* instead of wrapping all the `ParameterInit` subclasses, I just made their "constructors" factory methods on the `ParameterInit` companion object
* all of the bare `Expression`-generating functions are now members of the `Expression` companion object

I updated all the examples and most of the tests. Probably just by looking at the examples you can get a good flavor of how it would work.

--

This version should be much more idiomatic Scala (at the expense of having two levels of wrapper code), but I don't know that it "emphasizes the functional programming aspects", can you say more about what you mean by that?

Anyway, please let me know what you think of this approach, or if you see any changes that would make it nicer to use.

I explored a little more on geodesic convergence angle correction. If we use Lambert Conformal Conic, this can be calculate easily. For a radar station at (lat_r, lon_r) the angle correction is (lon_r - lon_0) * sin(lat_0), here lat_0, lon_0 are origin point(0,0) in the projection. Then we could apply this angle correction all all radar stations.

In terms of the geodesic distance between two radar station, a simple way is project two radars in to LCC then use Cartesian distance. Actually if we define standard parallels at 30N and 50N with original latitude at 40N, central meridian at 96W, the largest distance distortion will be around 2%. It is more accurate than using a constant earth radius (R=6371km) to calculate geodesic distance.

Since LCC is projectable and inverse-projectable, it will be easy to inverse-project any range gate back to lat-lon. When plotting, we could plot using Basemap. Then only concern is, there are so many gates to project and inverse project. This could be a performance bottleneck.

The term "production names" comes from Glyphs.app terminology. If a designer wants to keep so-called "friendly" or "nice" names in Glyphs.app, they have to set a custom parameter "Don't Use Production Names" in the Font Info.
This way, both Glyphs.app and fontmake will not rename the glyphs, i.e keep the friendly names.

Unless one tells fontmake to explicitly use `--production-names`, in which case one must conclude that for that particular run of fontmake the user wishes to rename the glyph names, whether or not the custom parameter is set in the .glyphs file.

The TXT processor is a degenerate CSV parser (with the delimiter being '\n', and no quoting or headers). So, we can always abuse tabulator to read these files - even without these code modifications (although we'll still need to be able to switch off html detection with an extra parameter).

DEMO:
```bash
$ cat > bla
Standing on the water casting your bread
While the eyes of the idol with the iron head are glowing.
Distant ships sailing into the mist,
You were born with a snake in both of your fists while a hurricane was blowing.
Freedom just around the corner for you
But with truth so far off, what good will it do?

Jokerman dance to the nightingale tune,
Bird fly high by the light of the moon,
Oh, oh, oh, Jokerman.
$ python
Python 3.6.1 (default, Mar 27 2017, 00:25:54)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.0.0 (clang-800.0.42.1)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import tabulator
>>> it=tabulator.Stream('bla', format='csv', delimiter='\n', headers=['data']).open().iter()
>>> list(it)
[['Standing on the water casting your bread'], ['While the eyes of the idol with the iron head are glowing.'], ['Distant ships sailing into the mist,'], ['You were born with a snake in both of your fists while a hurricane was blowing. '], ['Freedom just around the corner for you'], ['But with truth so far off, what good will it do?'], [], ['Jokerman dance to the nightingale tune,'], ['Bird fly high by the light of the moon,'], ['Oh, oh, oh, Jokerman.']]
```

Is this really abuse? A bit. 

- For some text files it actually makes lots of sense (e.g. https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dwyl/english-words/master/words.txt).
- For others it doesn't, although still useful (e.g. if you want to grep over a file it's nice to have a way to stream its lines even if it's remote).
- I agree that for many HTMLs it won't work, but for others it might be used to feed the parser incrementally (http://lxml.de/parsing.html#incremental-event-parsing). 

Do we need tabulator for that? Perhaps not all of it, although it's loader system is pretty useful (and a waste to rewrite).

I suggest that if this is possible to do it without adding a custom TXT processor (and it seems to be the case), then let's do it like this - we just need the HTML detection feature to be configurable.

I will concede that MISS is publishing some bad links on their page, but I can't really make sense of what their strategy is in general.  They seem to publish two records for each opinion, one with a working link url, and one with a broken link url.  This seems to be the pattern.  We are extracting both, and thus DO have access to the good link already.  I don't see any point in converting the broken links to match the good links that we already have.  If anything, we should contact the court and ask them why they are publishing broken links and what the point of this whole duplicate strategy is.

Apologies if this is all going over my head.

We already pass in an override transport class so that we sniff out of band of queries, but I'm slightly hesitant to override sniff_hosts since well, it's rather subtle (and important to get right) functionality.

To be honest this would be easy for us to override if we could control the order of the iteration [here](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/fb8eb4e38588d0ac582412f1acc020693260dd7e/elasticsearch/transport.py#L200). Maybe a python function or property that we could override? For example:

```python
def get_all_connections(self):
    return chain(self.connection_pool.connections, self.seed_connections)

# ...
# then in sniff_hosts
for c in self.get_all_connections():
    # do normal thing
```

Then for our use case we would just override all_connections to reverse the order.

The issue is affecting most of the third party SDK's right now, including java.  

Situation is best documented here: 
https://github.com/Azure/azure-xplat-cli/issues/2698

In short, VM's behave differently than other resources types right now, and it will continue to change.  The metrics for VM's are stored on azure storage tables, which means you have to use a completely different API/library to get the data.  As the article above says, powershell can get the metrics, but does so by invoking the .NET library for Azure storage. 

Then, this was recently announced: 
https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/event-hubs-streaming-azure-diags-data/

It seemed almost like diagnostics and metrics would only be available via eventhubs and storage, but I emailed one of the REST API developers about the issue with the /metrics endpoint and they said this: 

"we are working on the REST api to access metrics. They will be stored in memory, so it has much better latency.   You will also have the option to export these metrics to blobs and eventhubs."

I hope this helps somehow.
The API was [added](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/commit/6885b6c4161b0a4aa1983c8460e43dbb550fcf59) and will go out with the next release.

If I recall correctly, execution requests aren't lost because the kernel connection isn't established yet. Instead, they are never produced.

The options I use in node for the server piece is:

```js
const options = {
  key: process.env.SERVER_KEY,
  cert: process.env.SERVER_CERT,
  ca: process.env.CA,
  requestCert: true, 
  rejectUnauthorized: true,
};
```

The pertinent options for certs there are the key, cert, and a strict setting of only one CA - the one we the users control. As for strictness, it's these two options:

* `requestCert` - the server will request a certificate from clients that connect and attempt to verify the certificate
* `rejectUnauthorized` - the server will reject any connection which is not authorized with the configured CA. For the client, the server certificate is verified against the list of supplied CAs.

It's not clear to me what options we need in Tornado, seemed like I need to build an SSL Context via Python stdlib, exposing the options up to the notebook server.

@jmchilton maybe you will find some ideas in the following.

* Planemo: Galaxy tool development and testing made easy.
* Improve usability for Galaxy tool developers with the Planemo project.

Galaxy has gained over time a very adoptive system to describe tools. This system is capable of integrating the most complex tools into the Galaxy framework, yet it can be as simple as writing a few lines in a markup language to make simple tools accessible. Today the Galaxy Tool Shed contains ~3000 tools from different areas of computational research and a vibrant community is updating and improving these tools. 
Nevertheless, a highly flexible system can implement the same feature in several ways and can get complicated very fast. A community based commission (IUC) is maintaining a best practice guide [[1](https://galaxy-iuc-standards.readthedocs.org/en/latest/),[2](https://wiki.galaxyproject.org/Tools/BestPractices)] to tackle the first problem and define high quality standards for Galaxy Tools. Planemo is a command-line utility that aims to solve the complexity problem and can help a Galaxy Tool developer to stick to best-practice guidelines. The Planemo project is closely working with the IUC to adopt to recent changes, integrate new features to stay in sync with the Galaxy project and to make Tool development simple. 

-------------

Continues testing of tools is a very important way to ensure the high quality of produced Galaxy tools. Unfortunately, this was not simple and probably the most complicated part in tool development.  Planemo closes this gap by automatizing testing steps completely and offering convenient tools to spot errors immediately, generating and visualizing error reports. These can be used by individual developers as well as in continuous integration testing with TravisCI or Jenkins to test tools as soon as they are pushed into a repository. 

Overall Planemo will drastically improve the quality of tools, both from a developer point of view as from the  Galaxy user (by unify Interfaces).

----
The Galaxy project is constantly facing the challenge to improve accessibility and usability. This problem is hard on it's own but it makes it even harder if you have a diverse user base like the Galaxy community has. User's can be found in any community, from wet-lab scientists to programmers and shell lovers. One group is interested in working with a web interface to analyze data, the others are more interested to deploy their tools or talk to Galaxy via an API. Here we present the Planemo project that can enhance the usability of Galaxy in general by assisting tool developers with a command-line utility to create high-class Galaxy tools. This will lower the barrier for developers to integrate their tools into Galaxy and  increase usability for all other users by sticking and providing best practices, resulting in more unified interfaces.

[1] https://galaxy-iuc-standards.readthedocs.org/en/latest/
[2] https://wiki.galaxyproject.org/Tools/BestPractices

On Py2.7+, then yes, if the failure was that `coverage` was not found.
Rather than trying to work out why `coverage xml` failed, on Python 2.7+ the approach should be to check if coverage exists , then invoke `sys.executable`.

```python
try:
    import coverage
    try_to_run('sys.executable -m coverage xml')
except ImportError:
    try_to_run('coverage xml')
```

Py2.6 has problems with `-m` , and I am not sure that it can be worked around.

However. if you can import and use coverage within Python, rather than creating a subprocess, that would work on Py 2.6, be faster, and give greater control over how `coverage` failures.

Before release, how about we create a separate option `--group-by`, which is used when `--dist` is `load`? By default, `group-by` would be `none` which sends tests individually to workers, and `group-by=module` provides the functionality of this PR. Having a separate option would allow us to extend the option to support grouping by classes in the future.

So this:

```
pytest --dist=loadsuite ...
``` 

Becomes:

```
pytest --dist=load --group-by=module ...
```

I think I like option #1, "serve this log queue from a background thread, calling the callbacks on this thread. This will provide the snappiest log experience (minimal delay between logging and displaying the logs) but it also means there will be user callbacks called on a non-main thread which might trip people up.".

Anyone using this is only going to be feeding the logs into their own logging system, so it should not really trip people up that it is invoked in the context of a different thread than their main thread, I would think.

In terms of giving the app some control over the format, I don't know. Would it make sense for the app to provide a log object as returned by the python logging.getLogger()? That way the app could set up their formatter and log level any way they wanted, and confluent-kafka-python could just call log.info, log.debug, etc. Then there would be no need for callbacks.

I have never thought that the app_label is too important for django-salesforce, except that it must exist, because SFDC doesn't add the app name to the table name. If you use a Model with the same name in two app, it is possible though both models share the same table.

Models in django-salesforce are not authoritative for database structure, also because objects and fields are frequently locked in Salesforce by dependencies of Apex classes. I frequently use inspectdb and I'm preparing [dynamic fields](https://github.com/hynekcer/django-salesforce/wiki/Dynamic-fields). That is fields and models refreshed by inspectdb to some helper model and filtered by a custom condition to a current model or to models in more apps. That do you think about? The best code for it is in [this my branch](https://github.com/hynekcer/django-salesforce/tree/fix167) When I switch between production and sandbox I easily refresh the helper model.py and I have never a problem now that some field (used in model, but not used by app) was not found. It helps also that I don't have a problem that some field, that is not present in all instances, can not be used because it is not in the app model. If I develop in the console, I frequently use directly the full helper model obtained by inspectdb.

Most of terminal emulators do not bother having their own font rendering library, using the existing ones. AFAIK on linux there are two main libraries: freetype (which is able to use fallback fonts and does so by default, configured via XML files) and libXfont (X11 font rasterisation library that does not depend on freetype unlike libXft; not the best choice, but it should always be there). All terminals I know use freetype (even rxvt-unicode; at least they link with it and not with libXfont), but apparently do this rather differently: konsole happens to render fallback glyphs too wide to fit into a screen cell, producing more or less obtrusive visual bugs; rxvt-unicode somehow tells freetype to ignore all configured fallbacks, except for those explicitly provided in *terminal emulator* configuration; not sure about other terminals. As you may see in your second screenshot, watch symbol is not in terminus even though GNOME terminal is able to display it.

Dunno why you are able to see symbol used to indicate “blustery” weather, guess somebody configured rxvt-unicode to use fallback fonts, but did not configure to also use PowerlineSymbols as a fallback (or you would see arrows). But the fact that GNOME terminal and rxvt-unicode have different shape of flags means that they have different fallback font. This just shows that idea “print characters to determine whether font contains them” is poor by design, better take some specialized font viewer or editor (like fontforge) and check there instead.

```python
class JobList(MutableMapping, AsyncIterable, metaclass=abc.ABCMeta):
    """
    Describes an interface for manipulating a set of jobs posted to the API.
    The Job list should be iterating over all the jobs in the list.
    """
    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __getitem__(self, job_id: UUID) -> Job:
        """
        Given a job ID, this method must return a job with an ID
        corresponding to that job ID, or raise ``KeyError``.

        :param job_id: The ID of the job to retrieve, or the value of the
            job to retrieve
        :return: The job
        :raises: :exc:`KeyError` if a job with that ID does not exist
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __setitem__(self, job_id: UUID, job: Job) -> None:
        """

        :param job_id: The ID of the job to set
        :param job: The new job that is to occupy that ID
        :raises: :exc:`KeyError` if a job with that ID does not exist
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __delitem__(self, job_id: UUID) -> None:
        """

        :param job_id: The ID of the job to delete
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[Job]:
        """

        :return: An iterator that can iterate synchronously through all the
        jobs in the set
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __len__(self) -> int:
        """

        :return: The number of jobs in the set
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __contains__(self, job_id: Union[UUID, Job]) -> bool:
        """
        Membership test. This method MUST be able to check whether a Job or
        JobID is in the container

        :param job_id: The job or the Job ID for which membership is to be
            checked
        :return: ``True`` if the job belongs to this service
        """

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __aiter__(self) -> AsyncIterator[Job]:
        """

        :return: An iterator that can asynchronously iterate over all the
            jobs in the set
        """
        raise NotImplementedError()

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def __eq__(self, other: 'JobList') -> bool:
        """

        :param other: The other job list against which equality is to be
            determined
        :return: ``True`` if the equality definition is met, otherwise
            ``False``
        """

```

@alanfranz yes sockets rise a timeout exception when they time-out but if you do not reach the timeout, `recv` returns the bytes that are available and does not block until the number of bytes requested is received, that's what i was referring to (back then, more than a decade ago (14yrs), when the pySerial API was designed, Python 1.5.2 and 2.2 did not have a timeout on sockets). Anyway, there is no point in discussing, `socket.recv` as I only tried to explain a design decision for pySerial that was made a long time ago.

You can not easily compare sockets with serial ports. While sockets have a concept of closing a connection and the other end can be there or getting lost, there is no such clear concept for the serial port. While some appliances use the control lines to signal their presence, it's certainly not common to all devices. That's why serial `read()` returns just one byte and not "all" like `read`() on files does. There is no concept of an "end" that applies to all serial devices.

Comparing pipes (or files), that have no timeout, with serial ports that have one (even more than one ;-) also can not deliver a definitive answer.

If `read` would support raising an exception after a timeout, then a few problems arise. When there is already data received: does it throw away that data (bad) or attach the data to the `TimeoutException` (awkward) or does it successfully return the data on the first call, remember the error and raise the exception on the next call (probably surprising...)?

My concerns are that any of these alternatives would surprise the user more than the current solution and some solutions would add quite some complexity to the implementation of pySerial.

> it would sound much less awkward to me if it derived from `MultipleInputsLayer`

Same for me... Maybe we should have gone for (or still go for) `MultiLayer` instead of `MergeLayer`? But maybe that way it sounds awkward for every use case.

> I am fine with whichever you think works best.

I'm undecided about what's nicer. Deriving `RecurrentLayer` from `MergeLayer` would be the natural thing to do with the current API, but if we extend the API (or even just the available layers) for the multiple-output case, we can just as well use that. Let's compare code:

*Merge-layer variant:*
```python
class RecurrentLayer(MergeLayer):
    def __init__(self, incoming, mask=None, ...):
        incomings = [incoming]
        if mask is not None:
            incomings.append(mask)
        super(RecurrentLayer, self).__init__(incomings, **kwargs)
        # proceed as before
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shapes):
        input_shape = input_shapes[0]
        # proceed as before
    def get_output_for(self, inputs):
        input = inputs[0]
        mask = inputs[1] if len(inputs) > 1 else None
        # proceed as before

"""
data1 -> Conv -> Pool -> Conv -> Rec -> Rec -> Dense --\
mask1 ----------------------------^ -----^            Merge
data2 -> Conv -> Rec -> Rec -> Rec -> Dense -> Dense --/
mask2 ------------^ -----^ -----^
"""
leg1 = InputLayer(input_shape, data_var)
mask1 = InputLayer(input_shape, mask_var)
leg1 = Conv2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = Pool2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = Conv2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = RecurrentLayer(leg1, mask1, ...)
leg1 = RecurrentLayer(leg1, mask1, ...)
leg1 = DenseLayer(leg1, ...)
leg2 = InputLayer(input_shape2, data_var2)
mask2 = InputLayer(input_shape2, mask_var2)
leg2 = Conv2DLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, mask2, ...)
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, mask2, ...)
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, mask2, ...)
leg2 = DenseLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = DenseLayer(leg2, ...)
network = ElemwiseSumLayer([leg1, leg2])
```

*Multiple-inputs-as-dictionaries variant:*
```python
class MultiplexLayer(MergeLayer):
    def __init__(self, incomings, keys, **kwargs):
        super(MultiplexLayer, self).__init__(self, incomings, **kwargs)
        self.keys = keys
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shapes):
        return {key: shape for key, shape in zip(self.keys, input_shapes)}
    def get_output_for(self, inputs, **kwargs):
        return {key: input for key, input in zip(self.keys, inputs)}
combine = MultiplexLayer

class DemultiplexLayer(Layer):
    def __init__(self, incoming, key, **kwargs):
        super(DemultiplexLayer, self).__init__(self, incoming, **kwargs)
        self.key = key
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        return input_shape[self.key]
    def get_output_for(self, input):
        return input[self.key]
pick = DemultiplexLayer

class RecurrentLayer(Layer):
    def __init__(self, incoming, ...):
        super(RecurrentLayer, self).__init__(incoming, **kwargs)
        # proceed as before
    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        if isinstance(input_shape, dict):
            input_shape = input_shape['data']
        # proceed as before
    def get_output_for(self, input):
        if isinstance(input, dict):
            mask = input.get('mask')
            input = input['data']
        else:
            mask = None
        # proceed as before
        if mask is not None:
            return {'data': output, 'mask': mask}
        else:
            return output

"""
data1 -> Conv -> Pool -> Conv -> Multiplex -> Rec -> Rec -> Demultiplex -> Dense --\
mask1 -------------------------------^                                            Merge
data2 -> Conv -> Multiplex -> Rec -> Rec -> Rec -> Demultiplex -> Dense -> Dense --/
mask2 ---------------^
"""
leg1 = InputLayer(input_shape, data_var)
mask1 = InputLayer(input_shape, mask_var)
leg1 = Conv2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = Pool2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = Conv2DLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = combine([leg1, mask1], ['data', 'mask'])
leg1 = RecurrentLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = RecurrentLayer(leg1, ...)
leg1 = pick(leg1, 'data')
leg1 = DenseLayer(leg1, ...)
leg2 = InputLayer(input_shape2, data_var2)
mask2 = InputLayer(input_shape2, mask_var2)
leg2 = Conv2DLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = combine([leg2, mask2], ['data', 'mask'])
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = RecurrentLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = pick(leg2, 'data')
leg2 = DenseLayer(leg2, ...)
leg2 = DenseLayer(leg2, ...)
network = ElemwiseSumLayer([leg1, leg2])
```

Which one is nicer/shorter/more readable?

The promise of the `iterate_minibatches` function is to always return batches of `batchsize` items, and no half-filled batches. Your proposal would drop the last (half-filled) batch for datasets that have more than `batchsize` items, but return it for all smaller datasets. That's inconsistent. The current function always drops the last half-filled batch, even if it would have been the only one.
If anything, we could change the code to return half-filled batches for validation/testing, but for the MNIST example we've tailored the batch size to the size of the validation and test set to avoid this case altogether.
I'm leaning towards leaving such modifications to users basing their code on the MNIST example.

This was originally written to support replay in pywb, so a the WARC record is 'normalized' for replay, by adding http headers automatically to a resource or metadata or other record that doesn't generally have them. However, this most certainly should not be the default behavior in a general WARC reading library. The current behavior is handled by an else:
https://github.com/webrecorder/warcio/blob/bbfdb525866cabba6ede63ca10b793c8751a7ddb/warcio/recordloader.py#L172

Probably what it should do is have a `normalize_for_replay` or `add_default_httpheaders` flag or something like that which maintains the current behavior, or maybe it should be moved elsewhere altogether. I'll consider the options.

You send and receive text to/from Livy.

If we wanted that kind of interfacing across languages, we would need language parsers and the libraries installed in the Jupyter environment.

Some scenarios to think through:

* User wants to receive the Scala string `'res4: Double = 3.141728'` and have it convert to a Python double.
* User wants to receive a Spark object from the cluster back in the IPython kernel and does not have the Spark libraries installed.
* User wants to receive a Spark object from the cluster back in the IPython kernel and has a mismatched version of the Spark libraries installed.
* User wants to receive a {Scala/R/etc} Spark object from the cluster back in the IPython kernel and has a mismatched version of the Spark libraries installed.
* User wants to receive a `CustomLibraryObject` object from a Python library installed in the cluster back in the IPython kernel.
* User wants to receive a `CustomLibraryObject` object from a {Scala/R/etc} library installed in the cluster back in the IPython kernel.
* User wants to send a `CustomLibraryObject` object from a Python library installed in the Jupyter environment to the Spark context where the library is not available.

We should discuss how to do this and whether that's the paradigm we want to support or not.

> Simply, we can avoid this error by adding response.render() in RedirectsPanel

I believe this would still be a problem for `DebugToolbarMiddleware` for handling any other `SimpleTemplateResponse`/`TemplateResponse`. These types of responses could be returned by any view or middleware, not just django-debug-toolbar. It looks like `DebugToolbarMiddleware` never had support for delayed rendering. I'll add this with tests. I _think_ the correct approach is to render the content just before manipulating it:

```py
        # If the response supports deferred rendering and hasn't been rendered
        # yet, then ensure that it does get rendered before proceeding further.
        if hasattr(response, 'render') and callable(response.render):
            response.render()
        # Insert the toolbar in the response.
        content = force_text(response.content, encoding=response.charset)
```

Thanks for all the offers to help with Python 3 compatibility, it's pretty much appreciated. Actually the transition to Py3 is already started but the way I plan to go is through (I) move things out of nfcpy that don't necessarily belong there and (ii) gradually adapt smaller parts through normal releases. Under the first category fall NDEF message decoding and encoding (which will be replaced by nfcpy/ndeflib) and some tools that have grown to much in the examples section. The main reason for (ii) is that testing is by far the hardest part and can't be much automated. Any move to a separate branch for Py3 would lead to larger maintenance problems and I think the world is not yet ready for Py3 only.

Some things that would probably help to move faster:
- Patches for code updates that fix obvious Py3 issues and keep Py2 compatibility. Exposed APIs should be left untouched bust internal ones might change.
- Documentation examples that involve NDEF messages must change to use ndeflib and decode from Tag.ndef.octets. The ndef.Message.pretty() method used in quite some examples also needs a replacement.
- Ideas on how to distribute testing with all the different readers, tags and smartphones would be extremely helpful.
- Regression tests. There are some nose tests available but it's constantly falling behind.

```
import time
task:
```

will not work. You have to do

```
task:
  import time
  time.sleep(5)
```

Some networks (like mine) need to accept multiple inputs in `.forward` & do something like this:

```py
class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        # ...
    def forward(self, batch):
        firstInput = batch.firstInput
        secondInput = batch.secondInput
        # ...
```

Right now `fastai` assumes every `DataLoader` will yield a 2-tuple of tensors `(x, y)` & crashes if `x` is an arbitrary object that contains tensors because some functions in `model.py` (`fit`, `validate`, `validate_next`) try to convert that arbitrary object into a `torch.Variable` (by calling `V`, leading to `NotImplementedError` from `T`).

My code stops `V` crashing on lists/tuples with a depth of 2, (depth-2 because functions like `fit` wrap `x` in a list).

| Code                | Works now | Works after my change |
|---------------------|-----------|-----------------------|
| V(torch.Tensor)     | :heavy_check_mark:         | :heavy_check_mark:                     |
| V([torch.Tensor])   | :heavy_check_mark:         | :heavy_check_mark:                     |
| V([(torch.Tensor)]) | ❌         | :heavy_check_mark:                     |

Supporting this requires either one modification in `core.py` or several in `model.py`.

Instead of making `V` find tensors in the input and convert them to variables, we could rename `create_variable` to `maybe_create_variable` & make it behave like the identity function when `T` throws errors.

Hi there,

What is described in last paragraph does not look accurate. The quoting of the command passed in was put in exactly so users would *not* have to shell-escape the command string and there is a test for that [behaviour](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/blob/master/tests/test_pssh_ssh2_client.py#L775). This may well not work with non-posix shells though, that is true.

Further more, how a command is run varies depending on the SSH library used. With paramiko, no shell is used at all unless a command like `$SHELL <..>` is run. Running something like `echo` with paramiko without explicitly running in a shell would result in command not found.

OTOH, libssh2 seems to always use the user's defined shell whether or not an interactive shell was requested. So it would make sense to remove the `$SHELL` prefix and command quoting on libssh2 only, but on paramiko it would result in a broken client. 

Can test that out and if tests pass it can be removed.

To clarify, I think there's two big reasons to have the cli tools in addition to the python api, which we should emphasize in the design:

1. Convenience: not needing to fire up a python environment when working in a shell. Most housekeeping/setup tasks are done from shell rather than python in our current docs & usage.
2. Ability to easily use these tools together with the rest of the cli/bash/excel/etc software ecosystem. Many of the current output formats are currently not very convenient for piping to other tools. Ability to parse/extract values from the output is a plus. The dataset commands have a `--format` specifier for getting the output in parse-able formats like csv, which traditionally Ops used quite heavily.

After discussions with @jeremyh I have updated code and pull request to default to `auto` mode as opposed to `exact`. The `exact` mode while producing the same query as before would behave in unexpected way on migrated stacked files, `auto` mode will produce different query, but will appear identical in behaviour as before, for the exception of now also working on directories and not just files.

Regarding Step 1, am I right in thinking we don't really want a new thread waiting for every subprocess? I'm imagining one thread calling `WaitForMultipleObjects` on H handles where H = P processes + C cancel contexts + 1 control event. The control event wakes the thread to add more subprocess handles and resume waiting. Does that sound feasible?

> Just to be sure, these don't count as deep internals? from `._core._windows_cffi import ffi, kernel32, ErrorCodes, raise_winerror, _handle`

Nope! The raw Win32 bindings aren't connected to Trio's internals at all. In a perfect world they'd be part of the stdlib (similar to how the `os` module has bindings for lots of low-level POSIX APIs), or they could be trivially moved into a third-party library. For now there's no advantage to doing that, and they need to live somewhere, so they're in `trio/_core` next to `_io_windows.py`, which was the only user. But using them in `WaitForSingleObject` doesn't create any coupling between your code and Trio's internals.

> However, I have note seen module docstrings elsewhere, is there a reason that Trio avoids them?

Mostly I just don't use them myself and forget to write them :-). I guess another reason is that my manuals tend to have a lot more narrative text around each module than I would want to put in a module docstring, and keeping module docstrings up to date is a hassle. But I don't have any objection to adding docstrings to public module that are like, a few sentences long and give a brief overview of what the module's purpose is.

> This new feature is Windows only. I've added this to the function docstring. What to do wrt exposing? I now wrote it such that the function is only in hazmat on Windows, but maybe its better to always have it present and just fail on non-windows?

There are arguments either way, but for now Trio follows the stdlib's lead here and makes it so that system-specific functions only exist where they're supported. It's kind of convenient because you can do `if hasattr(trio.hazmat, "WaitForSingleObject"): ...` and select the appropriate system-specific code path without having to guess what's supported or try actually invoking the function.

> I checked, and these names are initially in `__all__`, but are dropped (on Windows): `wait_writable`, `wait_readable`, `notify_fd_close`, `current_kqueue`, `monitor_kevent`, `wait_kevent`. These do not sound platform specific,

They are all platform specific. The `wait_writable` and `wait_readable` should really be called `wait_fd_writable` and `wait_fd_readable`, and Windows doesn't have functions that work on arbitrary fd's the same way Unixes do. And the kqueue and kevent functions are related to the BSD/MacOS async I/O primitive [`kqueue`](https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?kqueue).

> in fact, for some I cannot find any definition. Are these artifacts?

It's, umm, like you said, not the prettiest part of Trio. They're defined in `trio/_core/_io_*.py`, and exported via a convoluted chain of code that involves the `@_public` decorator, some code generation at import time in the bottom of `trio/_core/_run.py`, some code in `trio/_core/__init__.py`, etc.

> has it been considered to do this differently?

Yeah, this dates back to the very beginning of trio, and has turned out to be pretty overengineered. We really should simplify it, which is #542.
